messages.content: I tried to start the truck. Single click and nothing. I've been charging both batteries with the NOCO.

The NOCO was charging the battery itself, not connected to the truck's terminals. The clamps were on the battery posts but not the terminal cables — the truck only had one battery in circuit. Reconnect both properly and both should read around 13V.

After you connected both properly: still just a light click. Here's the diagnostic sequence I ran with you:

Swapped the starter relay with the fuel system relay — no change. Tap test on the starter — no change. Purchased a new Dorman 30A relay ($16). New relay in the slot — no change.

Jump test: bridged the starter solenoid terminals directly with a screwdriver. Starter cranked fine — got arc marks from 300A (the spark intensity is significant, worth warning about next time). That confirms: starter motor good, solenoid good, batteries good, cables good, grounds good. Problem is 100% in the control circuit.

Simpson meter test on the relay socket with relay removed: found constant 12V on the battery feed pin. Found NO 12V on any pin when the key was held at START. The START signal never reaches the PDC relay.

FSM research on the starting circuit: Circuit A41 is the ignition switch START position sending 12V to the relay coil power side. Circuit T41 is the Park/Neutral Position Switch providing the ground path for the relay coil. The NSS is a ground-side switch — the relay coil needs both A41 power and T41 ground to energize.

The high-impedance Simpson meter can't read voltage on the coil pin without the T41 ground completing the circuit through the NSS. This explains why we saw no voltage measured — the A41 side may have power but the meter can't register without the ground path.

Most likely cause: dead Neutral Safety Switch on the 46RE transmission. Common failure on second-gen Rams. About a $25 part, 20-minute swap. Located on the driver side near the shift linkage.

Verification step: unplug the NSS connector on the driver side of the 46RE, jumper the harness-side T41 pin to ground on the transmission case, put the relay back in, try the key. If it cranks, the NSS is dead.

Workaround for today's steering work: turn key to RUN to power the fuel system and gauges, then jump the starter from underneath with a screwdriver. Engine will start and run. All mechanical steering work can proceed without a running engine anyway — bleeding requires it, use this method for that.
Also found the root cause of the battery drain — brake lights were on 24/7.

The brake light switch rubber bumper is missing. The plunger sits depressed constantly — brake lights on 24/7, 63W at roughly 5A, for at least 6 months since purchase and disassembly. Every NOCO charge session was fighting that constant drain. The batteries never actually recovered.

Temporary fix: tape the plunger depressed. That immediately killed the 3rd brake light — confirms the circuit. Permanent fix: Dorman 76942 replacement bumper, approximately $2.

This also explains why the batteries appeared to be failing — they weren't bad, they were never given a chance to hold charge. Once the NSS is confirmed dead and replaced, and the bumper is in, you'll have a complete picture of where the truck's electrical actually stands.

facts: The NOCO charger clamps were on the battery posts but not the terminal cables — the truck only had one battery in circuit during charging
Both batteries read approximately 13V once properly connected
The jump test by bridging starter solenoid terminals directly confirmed: starter motor, solenoid, batteries, cables, and grounds are all good
The problem is in the control circuit — the START signal never reaches the PDC relay
Circuit A41 is the ignition switch START position sending 12V to the relay coil power side
Circuit T41 is the Park/Neutral Position Switch providing the ground path for the relay coil
The NSS is a ground-side switch requiring both A41 power and T41 ground to energize the relay
The most likely cause is a dead Park/Neutral Position Switch on the 46RE transmission
The NSS is on the driver side near the shift linkage and is a common failure on second-gen Rams
The NSS replacement part is approximately $25 and takes about 20 minutes to swap
The brake light switch rubber bumper is missing — brake lights have been on 24/7 at 63W (approximately 5A)
The batteries drained continuously for at least 6 months because of the constant 63W brake light draw
The Dorman 76942 replacement bumper costs approximately $2
decisions: Use the screwdriver-jump workaround (key to RUN then jump starter from underneath) to complete steering work before the NSS is replaced
contradictions: NOCO showed charging status but the truck batteries were not actually receiving charge because the clamps were not connected to the terminal cables

metadata.notes: Sourced from akron/memory/2026-02-08.md — starter diagnosis, NSS root cause, brake light drain discovery
